sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच
सन्न्यासं कर्मणां कृष्ण पुनर्योगं च शंससि ।
यच्छ्रेय एतयोरेकं तन्मे ब्रूहि सुनिश्चितम् ॥ १ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
arjuna uvāca
sannyāsaṁ karmaṇāṁ kṛṣṇa punar yogaṁ ca śaṁsasi |
yac chreya etayor ekaṁ tan me brūhi su-niścitam || 1 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Arjuna said: O Kṛṣṇa, first of all You ask me to renounce work, and then again You recommend work with devotion. Now will You kindly tell me definitely which of the two is more beneficial?
Arjuna said: O Krishna, first of all You ask me to renounce work, and then again You recommend work with devotion. Now will You kindly tell me definitely which of the two is more beneficial?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1592 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In this Fifth Chapter of the Bhagavad-gītā, the Lord says that work in devotional service is better than dry mental speculation. Devotional service is easier than the latter because, being transcendental in nature, it frees one from reaction.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.1 (O Krsna,) samsasi, You praise, i.e. speak of; sannyasam, renunciation; karmanam, of actions, of performance of various kinds of rites enjoined by the scriptures; punah ca, and again; You praise yogam, yoga, the obligatory performance of those very rites! Therefore I have a doubt as to which is…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-1-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 5.1 as Arjuna voicing a doubt born of apparently contradictory teaching: in earlier chapters Krishna has praised both renunciation of action (sannyāsa of karmas, in chapter 2 and 3.4) and the obligatory performance of action (karma-yoga, 3.8 etc.). Arjuna asks for one of the two to be definitely commended as the better, given they look opposed. Shankara emphasises that the question is sincere — it is not a hostile challenge but a request from the student who has earned the right to a verdict.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.1 Arjuna said — ‘You praise the renunciation of actions, i.e., Jnana Yoga at one time, and next Karma Yoga’. This is what is objected to: In the second chapter, you have said that Karma Yoga alone should be practised first by an aspirant for release; and that the vision of the self should be achie…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-1-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja groups 5.1 as Arjuna's objection: 'You praise the renunciation of action, i.e., jñāna-yoga, at one time, and karma-yoga at another.' He notes Arjuna's earlier confusion (in 3.1-3.2) and his current pressing question — these two are means independent of each other in Vishishtadvaita's reading; Arjuna is asking which is the *uniquely* better path for him, not whether both are valid in principle.

positions.rendering: work in devotion / devotional service (Gaudiya emphasis)
karma-yoga, the obligatory performance of scripturally enjoined action
karma-yoga as one of two independent means alongside jñāna-yoga

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Prabhupada's 'work in devotion' reflects the Gaudiya rendering of yoga as bhakti-tinged karma-yoga; Shankara reads yoga here narrowly as obligatory karma performed without attachment (yoga as viparyaya of sannyāsa); Ramanuja reads it as karma-yoga, one of two soteriological paths, with the second being jñāna-yoga (which he then identifies with sannyāsa here).

literal_meaning: Arjuna said: O Krishna, you praise the renunciation of actions, and again you praise yoga (karma-yoga). Tell me decisively: which one of these two is more beneficial?
traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna opens chapter 5 with a sharp question. Having heard Krishna praise both sannyāsa (in chapter 3 verses on the path of jñāna-niṣṭhā) and karma-yoga (in chapter 3.8 and elsewhere), he experiences the teaching as containing two recommendations that can look mutually exclusive, and asks for a decisive verdict. The verse opens the chapter dialectically — the student presses the teacher for one answer over the other, having earned the right to do so by chapter 4's accumulated trust.
